concepts.title: Stability and structure of alkenes
concepts.conceptBody:
Alkenes

Structure

Reactivity

  • A $\pi $ bond is a region of high electron density so alkenes are typically nucleophiles.
  • Alkenes typically undergo addition reactions in which the $\pi $ bond is converted to two new $\sigma $ bonds.

Stability

There are 3 factors that influence alkene stability:

1. Degree of substitution: more highly alkylated alkenes are more stable, so tetra > tri > di > mono-substituted.

2. Stereochemistry: trans > cis due to reduced steric interactions when $\text{R}$ groups are on opposite sides of the double bond.

3. Conjugated alkenes are more stable than isolated alkenes.

4. Degree of Unsaturation

$\text{DU}=\left( \frac{2{{\text{n}}_{1}}+2-{{\text{n}}_{2}}}{2} \right)$

where ${{\text{n}}_{1}}=\text{ no}\text{. of C-atom}$

${{\text{n}}_{2}}=\text{ no}\text{. of H atom}$

Note: (1) If a halogen is attached, then take halogen as H atom

(2) If an oxygen atom is present we do not count it.

(3) If nitrogen is present, for every N atom deduct 1 H atom.

(4) If degree of unsaturation $=2$ then there can be:

(a) 2 double bonds

(b) 1 triple bond

(c) one double bond + one cyclic ring

Example 

A molecule for ${{\text{C}}_{s}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}$ does not show stereoisomerism. Upon hydrogenation, it absorbs 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$. Upon reductive or oxidative ozonysis it gives some symmetrical diketone. Deduce the structure of $A$.

Solution: 

$\text{DU}$$=\left( \frac{2{{\text{n}}_{1}}+2-{{\text{n}}_{2}}}{2} \right)$ 

${{\text{n}}_{1}}$ $=8$

${{\text{n}}_{2}}$ $=12$

$\therefore $ $\text{DU}$ $=\frac{16+2-12}{2}=3$

Now, since it absorbs 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$, hence only one double bond is present. And since on reductive or oxidative ozonlysis it gives same diketone, therefore the double bond is of the form of

Hence, there is one possibility i.e.

General points regarding stability of rings :

(1) \[\Delta \to \] unstable due to angle strain

(2) Stability increases with increase in the size of ring upto cyclohexane.

(3) Chair form of cyclohexane is the most stable.

concepts.title: Hydrogenation of alkynes
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation Of Alkenes

From alkynes

(1) Hydrogenation of alkynes 

Reduction of alkynes is a useful method for the stereoselective synthesis of disubstituted alkene is desired, hydrogenation in the presence of Lindlar’s catalyst is commonly used, though hydroboration followed by hydrolysis provides an alternative approach. Reduction of the alkyne by sodium metal in liquid ammonia gives the trans-alkene.

concepts.title: Types of elimination reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation by elimination reaction

 Dehydrohalogenation 

An elimination is the loss of two atoms or groups from a molecule, which will typically result in the formation of a new $\pi $ bond. E.g.

IUPAC name : 1-2 elimination

commonname : $\beta$-elimination

Types of elimination Reaction

(1) ${{\mathbf{E}}_{1}}$ elimination 

This is an unimolecular elimination. Which means that RDS (Rate Determining step) involves only one molecule. Also it is the slow ionization to generate a carbocation.

The second step is the fast removal of a proton by the solvent.

In general for on ${{\text{E}}_{1}}$ reaction:

Step 1: Carbocation formation (slow, rate determining)

Step 2: Abstraction of proton (fast)

(2) Second order elimination $\mathbf{(}{{\mathbf{E}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{)}$

Generally this elimination occurs with a strong base, which elimates a proton more quickly than the substrate can ionize.

${{\text{E}}_{2}}$ elimination is bimolecular and takes place in only one step.

Here solvation will release solvation energy.

Solvation energy $+\pi $ bond formation energy is enough to break the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond.

concepts.title: Saytzeff rule of addition
concepts.conceptBody:
Saytzeff’s Rule

In this type of elimination reaction the two leaving groups are lost from the adjacent carbon atoms. One of the leaving groups is the halogen atom and the $\text{C}$-atom from which the halogen and the C-atom from which the halogen is lost is usually designated as 1 or ( $\alpha $ ) carbon atom. Hydrogen, the other leaving group is lost from the neighbouring carbon which is designated as 2 or $(\beta )$ carbon atom. Now, if the alkyl halide has two or more $\beta $-carbon atoms, two or more alkenes are possible. According to saytzeff’s rule, dehydro halogenation of alkyl halides leads to the formation of that alkene leads the major product which has maximum number of alkyl groups attached to

For example

Note: • Use Sayteff rule only in E2 reaction, not in E1 to predict the product.

• That product will be formed in major amount which has more stable alkene.

In an ${{\text{E}}_{1}}$ reaction, the rate determining step is the loss of the leaving group to form the intermediate carbocation. The more stable the carbocation is, the easier it is to form, and the faster the ${{E}_{1}}$ reaction will be. Some student fall into the trap of thinking that the system with the less stable carbocation will react fastest, but they are forgetting that it is the generation of the carbocation that is rate determining.

Since carbocation intermediates are formed during an ${{\text{E}}_{1}}$, there is always the possibility of rearrangements (e.g. 1, 2-hydride or 1, 2-alkyl shift) to generate a more stable carbocation.

However, if the size of base is increased, it finds it relatively easier to abstract proton from a less substituted $\beta $-carbon atom than from more substituted $\beta $-carbon atom of alkyl halide. Therefore, less stable alkene becomes the major product. This is known as Hoffmann’s rule .

For example

In place of haloalkanes, suphonyl derivatives of alkanes can also be used in the base catalysed elimination reaction for the preparation of alkenes.

Hoffman elimination also takes place when the leaving group is poor (In general strong bases are poor leaving groups). 

HF < HCl < HBr < HI

${{\text{F}}^{-}}>\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}>\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}>{{\text{I}}^{-}}$

Example 

Predict the alkene formed in each case.

concepts.title: By vicinal dehalogenation
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation by Vicinal dehalogenation

Dehalogenation of vicinal dihalides can be effected either by $\text{NaI}$ in acetone or by zinc in presence of acetic acid or ethanol.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{CHBr}-\text{CHBr}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow[C{{H}_{3}}COOH\,\,or\,{{C}_{2}}{{H}_{5}}OH]{Zn\,\,dust}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-CH=CH-C{{H}_{3}}$

$C{{H}_{3}}-CHBr-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Br}\xrightarrow[acetone]{NaI}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{CH}=\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

The reaction mechanism involves loss of two halogen atoms in two steps. The two halogen atoms align themselves at 180° in the same plane before they are lost.

(i) With NaI in acetone

(ii) With zinc dust

$\text{Zn}\to \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

 Acid Catalysed Dehydration of Alcohols

Alkenes can be prepared by dehydrogenation of alcohol in presence of protonating acid such as ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ etc.

with 3° alcohol, reaction always occurs by ${{E}_{1}}$ mechanism.

Example 

Mechanism: 

Reaction occurs in 3 steps

(1) Protonation : Attack by \[{{H}^{+}}\]ion on $-\text{OH}$ group

(2) Dehydration : Removal of water & formation of carbocation slow step

  • Carbocation can rearrange according to stability

(3) Deprotonation $\to $ Removal of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ ion
